section.title: To the Church in Ephesus
section.sectionRationale: The first of seven letters, each following an identical formula drawn from the vision of chapter 1: address, self-description from that vision, commendation, complaint, remedy, promise, and a call to hear. Ephesus's letter establishes this pattern and the recurring promise to the one who overcomes, repeated with variation through the end of chapter 3.
section.thesis: To a hardworking, doctrinally vigilant church that has abandoned its first love, the Christ who walks among the lampstands calls for remembrance, repentance, and a return to former deeds, promising the fruit of the tree of life in the Paradise of God to the one who overcomes.
section.frameNote: Two units: Christ's self-description and his commendation of Ephesus's labor and discernment (1-3); the charge of abandoned first love, the call to repent, and the promise to the one who overcomes (4-7).

verses.text: “To the {{aggelos|angel}} of the church in Ephesus write: These are the words of Him who holds the seven stars in His right hand and walks among the seven golden lampstands.
I know your {{ergon|deeds}}, your labor, and your perseverance. I know that you cannot tolerate those who are evil, and you have tested and exposed as liars those who falsely claim to be apostles.
Without growing weary, you have persevered and endured many things for the sake of My name.

units.label: Complaint, remedy, and promise
units.note: you have abandoned your first love

verses.text: But I have this against you: You have abandoned your {{prote_agape|first love}}.
Therefore, keep in mind how far you have fallen. Repent and perform the deeds you did at first. But if you do not repent, I will come to you and remove your lampstand from its place.
But you have this to your credit: You hate the works of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate.
He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches. To {{ho_nikon|the one who overcomes}}, I will grant the right to eat from the tree of life in the {{paradeisos|Paradise}} of God.

aggelos.gloss: Messenger, angel. Each letter is addressed to 'the angel' of its church, variously understood as a guardian angel, the congregation personified, or the human messenger or leader who represents and answers for that church before Christ.

ergon.gloss: Works, deeds. Every one of the seven letters opens 'I know your works': the Christ who walks among the lampstands watches not mere profession but the actual fruit, labor, and endurance of each congregation's life.

prote_agape.gloss: First love, the ardent, wholehearted devotion of the church's earliest days. Ephesus kept sound doctrine and hard labor, yet let this love grow cold, the single charge Christ brings against an otherwise commendable church.

ho_nikon.gloss: The one who overcomes, conquers, prevails. Every letter closes with a promise 'to the one who overcomes,' language echoed in 1 John 5:4-5, where the victory in view is simply persevering faith in Jesus.

paradeisos.gloss: Paradise, a loanword for a walled garden or royal park. It recalls Eden and anticipates the tree of life restored in the new creation of Revelation 22:2, promised here to the one who overcomes.

basic.context: This is the first of seven short letters Christ dictates to John for real churches in Roman Asia. Each follows the same pattern: Christ describes himself using an image from chapter 1's vision, commends what is good, names a complaint, calls for repentance, and promises a reward to 'the one who overcomes.' Ephesus is commended for hard work, perseverance, and testing false teachers, but rebuked for one thing: they have abandoned their first love.
basic.hermeneutics: The plain sense is a real letter to a real, historically known church, Ephesus, founded by Paul and later pastored by Timothy and, by tradition, John himself. Some interpreters additionally read the seven churches as picturing seven successive ages of church history, though most agree the primary and safest reading takes them as seven real congregations representing spiritual conditions any church, in any era, can fall into.
basic.originalLanguages: Every letter opens 'I know your erga,' your works, and Ephesus's works were real: labor, perseverance, discernment against false apostles. But they abandoned their prote agape, their first love. The promise is to ho nikon, the one who overcomes, who will eat from the tree of life in the paradeisos, the Paradise, of God.
basic.typology: The tree of life recalls Eden, barred to Adam and Eve in Genesis 3:24, and its promised restoration in Revelation 22:2 for all who overcome. Christ's self-description, walking among the lampstands, fulfills the imagery of Zechariah 4's golden lampstand, God's presence and light dwelling among his people.
basic.christ: Christ presents himself here exactly as John saw him in chapter 1, holding the seven stars, walking among the lampstands, present and attentive to his church's actual condition. He commends real labor and doctrinal courage, yet he weighs the heart behind the works and finds cooling love, showing that faithful activity without love falls short of what he asks.
basic.god: God is patient but not indifferent: he commends true good works honestly, and just as honestly names the deeper problem beneath busy religious life. The warning to remove the lampstand shows real consequences are possible even for an outwardly impressive church, while the promise of the tree of life shows his reward is generous and certain for those who overcome.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3:22-24 bars access to the tree of life after the fall. Revelation 22:2 restores it in the new creation. 1 John 4:19-21 ties love for God to love for one another. Ephesians, Paul's earlier letter to this same church, praises their faith and love, showing what may have since cooled.
basic.oneStory: The very first letter of Revelation warns that faithful labor can survive even as love for Christ quietly cools, and calls the church back to remember, repent, and return. It closes with a promise stretching back to Eden and forward to the new creation: the tree of life, lost in the garden, restored at last to everyone who overcomes in Christ.

context.literary: The first of seven letters sharing an identical sevenfold pattern: address, self-description, commendation, complaint, remedy, promise, and the call 'he who has an ear, let him hear.' Ephesus's letter establishes this template that the following six letters vary and develop.
context.historical: Ephesus was a major port city and the leading church of Roman Asia, founded through Paul's extended ministry there (Acts 19) and later associated with Timothy and John. It was known for confronting false teaching, as this letter itself commends, but also for the pressures of long-established, perhaps routine, religious life.
context.canonical: This letter's warning about losing first love while maintaining outward orthodoxy and labor echoes prophetic warnings against ritual without heart (Isaiah 29:13, Hosea 6:6) and anticipates Jesus's own teaching that not all who say 'Lord, Lord' truly know him (Matthew 7:21-23).

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend Ephesus's real doctrinal vigilance and hard labor honestly, while naming the deeper problem of cooling devotion to Christ, calling the church to remember, repent, and return to their first works, with both a warning and a promise attached.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The letter describes Ephesus's specific condition; it is not a universal claim that every hardworking church has necessarily lost its first love. Its abiding pattern is the call to examine whether outward faithfulness is matched by inward devotion, and to repent where it is not.

debates.question: What exactly is the 'first love' Ephesus is charged with abandoning?

views.label: Love for Christ himself
views.case: The letter's whole frame is Christ's own relationship to the church; the charge most naturally reads as cooling personal devotion to him, even while doctrine and discipline remained intact.

views.label: Love for one another within the congregation
views.case: Some read the cooling love primarily in relational terms, noting that vigorous doctrinal watchfulness, tolerating no evil and testing every claim, can curdle into suspicion and coldness toward fellow believers.

debates.bearing: Scripture rarely separates the two loves cleanly (1 John 4:19-21), so the remedy, remember, repent, and do the first works, likely addresses devotion to Christ and to his people together, not one to the exclusion of the other.

debates.question: Do the seven churches, beginning with Ephesus, also represent seven successive ages of church history?

views.label: Primarily seven real, historical churches
views.case: The letters are addressed to actual, named, geographically locatable congregations with specific, identifiable problems, and most interpreters across traditions read them first and foremost as real pastoral correspondence.

views.label: Also a prophetic outline of church history
views.case: A historicist strand of interpretation has long read the seven letters, from Ephesus's apostolic-era coolness to Laodicea's end-time lukewarmness, as a panoramic sketch of successive periods of church history.

debates.bearing: Nearly every tradition affirms the primary, historical address to real churches; the epochal reading is a minority overlay that should never be allowed to eclipse the direct, pastoral force these words carry for any congregation, in any era, that recognizes its own condition here.

study.originalLanguages: The letter opens with Christ's familiar refrain, 'I know your erga,' your works, naming Ephesus's real labor, perseverance, and doctrinal discernment. Yet the charge against them concerns their prote agape, their first love, now abandoned. The closing promise belongs to ho nikon, the one who overcomes, who will eat from the tree of life in the paradeisos, the Paradise, of God, a word that carries all the resonance of Eden regained.

typology.type: The tree of life, barred in Genesis 3:22-24
typology.fulfillment: Restored and offered here, and fully realized in the new creation of Revelation 22:2, to everyone who overcomes.
Fulfilled in Christ walking among the seven golden lampstands, God's presence and light dwelling among his gathered people.

study.christ: Christ addresses Ephesus exactly as John saw him, holding the seven stars and walking among the lampstands, present and watchful over his church's actual life, not merely its reputation. He commends genuine labor and courage against false teaching, yet presses past outward performance to the heart, naming cooling love as the deeper danger, and offering, to whoever overcomes, the restored fruit of the tree of life.
study.god: God's evaluation here is neither harshly dismissive nor merely flattering: he names real good honestly and real fault honestly in the same breath. The warning to remove the lampstand shows he takes cooling devotion seriously enough to threaten a church's very existence as a light-bearing witness, while the promise of Paradise regained shows his reward for faithful repentance is nothing less than Eden restored.

crossReferences.note: Access to the tree of life is barred after the fall, the loss this letter's promise reverses.
The tree of life is fully restored in the new creation at the book's close.
Love for God and love for fellow believers are bound together, bearing on what 'first love' includes.
Paul's extended founding ministry in Ephesus, the church now addressed generations later.

study.oneStory: The very first letter to the churches warns that a congregation can keep sound doctrine, hard labor, and real courage against error, and still let its first love for Christ quietly cool. Christ calls Ephesus to remember, repent, and return, promising the one who overcomes the very thing lost in Eden: free access to the tree of life, in the Paradise of God, the whole story's hope of restored communion with God finally regained.

sermon.title: Ephesus, Church that Fell
sermon.why: Ferguson works directly through Revelation 2:1-7, drawing out how a doctrinally sound, hardworking church can nonetheless fall through losing its first love for Christ, and what repentance and the promised tree of life mean for such a church.

quiz.q: What does Christ commend the Ephesian church for?

options.text: Their labor, perseverance, and discernment in testing and exposing false apostles.
options.note: Verses 2-3 name these specific commendable qualities.

options.text: Only their large size and wealth as a city church.
options.note: The commendation is about spiritual labor and discernment, not size or wealth.

options.text: Their tolerance of every teacher who came to them.
options.note: The opposite; they are praised for refusing to tolerate false apostles.

options.text: Nothing; the letter is entirely a rebuke.
options.note: Verses 2-3 and 6 give real, specific commendation before the complaint.

quiz.q: What single charge does Christ bring against Ephesus?

options.text: They have abandoned their first love.
options.note: Verse 4 names this as the one thing Christ holds against them.

options.text: They have completely abandoned sound doctrine.
options.note: Their doctrinal vigilance is actually commended, not condemned; the charge is cooling love.

options.text: They have joined the Nicolaitans' teaching.
options.note: The letter says they hate the works of the Nicolaitans, to their credit, not that they joined them.

options.text: They have stopped meeting together entirely.
options.note: The letter addresses their ongoing life as a congregation, not an absence of gathering.

quiz.q: What remedy does Christ prescribe for Ephesus's condition?

options.text: Remember how far they have fallen, repent, and perform the deeds they did at first.
options.note: Verse 5 gives this exact threefold remedy.

options.text: Simply work harder at the same activities they were already doing.
options.note: The remedy centers on repentance and returning to first works, not merely increasing existing activity.

options.text: Leave Ephesus and start a new congregation elsewhere.
options.note: The call is to repentance within the existing church, not relocation.

options.text: There is no remedy offered, only judgment.
options.note: A clear remedy and a promise are both offered alongside the warning.

quiz.q: What is promised to 'the one who overcomes' in verse 7?

options.text: The right to eat from the tree of life in the Paradise of God.
options.note: This is the exact promise given at the letter's close.

options.text: Immediate exemption from all future hardship.
options.note: The promise is specifically the tree of life in Paradise, not a guarantee against present trials.

options.text: Political rule over the city of Ephesus.
options.note: The reward is spiritual and eschatological, the tree of life, not earthly political power.

options.text: Nothing; overcomers receive no reward.
options.note: A specific, generous reward is explicitly promised.

quiz.q: What warning does Christ attach if Ephesus does not repent?

options.text: He will come and remove their lampstand from its place.
options.note: Verse 5 states this warning directly.

options.text: He will immediately destroy the entire city of Ephesus.
options.note: The warning concerns the church's lampstand, its witness, not the city's destruction.

options.text: He will replace their leaders with the Nicolaitans.
options.note: The warning is about removing the lampstand, unrelated to installing the Nicolaitans.

options.text: No warning is given, only encouragement.
options.note: A real, specific warning is attached to the call to repent.

questions: Ephesus kept sound doctrine and worked hard, yet had let its first love for Christ cool. Where might busyness or vigilance in your own life be quietly crowding out simple love for him?
Christ says 'remember, repent, and do the deeds you did at first.' What were the deeds of your own first love, and what would it look like to return to them now?
The church is commended for testing false teachers and refusing to tolerate evil. How do you hold discernment and love together, without letting one crowd out the other?
The promise to the one who overcomes is the tree of life, lost in Eden and restored in the new creation. How does that long, whole-Bible promise shape how you read your own present struggles?
Every letter ends, 'he who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches.' What do you sense the Spirit saying to you personally through this letter to Ephesus?

translationNotes.note: 'Your first love' can also be rendered 'the love you had at first,' pointing to an earlier state of devotion rather than a single past event.

'The Nicolaitans' is left untranslated as a proper name; their precise identity and teaching are not fully certain, though the letters treat their influence as a serious compromise the churches must reject.
'The one who overcomes' (ho nikon) can also be rendered 'the one who conquers' or 'the victor,' language that recurs with variation at the close of all seven letters.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the project's makor-scripture toolkit for Revelation 2:1-7. No em or en dashes appeared in the source verses for this section. Sermon link for Sinclair Ferguson's 'Ephesus, Church that Fell' verified live this session on thegospelcoalition.org, covering this exact passage.
